prompts: In the electrolysis of water, an electrolytic cell consists of electrodes: an anode and a cathode, submerged in water containing an electrolyte like sulfuric acid to enhance conductivity. A power source applies an electric current. At the anode, water molecules are oxidized, releasing oxygen gas and protons. At the cathode, protons gain electrons to form hydrogen gas. Hydrogen and oxygen gases are collected separately at each electrode. This setup efficiently decomposes water, producing hydrogen and oxygen in a 2:1 ratio, reflective of water's chemical composition (H₂O).
